titleLines: Running this is a real job.
opener.lead: What to say when someone pushes back. What to do when the room runs away from itself. How to leave a record that's worth reading six months from now.
opener.leadNote: Read once before session 1. Come back when something's going sideways.

toc.label: Things your team will ask you

runbook.title: How to run the hour.
runbook.lead: Each session is sixty minutes. The shape stays the same even when the content changes. Five blocks. Set them, hit them, the session moves.

blocks.title: Open with the converge.
blocks.body: Before any AI-heavy exercise, set the constraint out loud. "In twenty minutes you're going to pick one. Generate as much as you want. At the twenty-minute mark we go around the room and each person names their pick." One sentence prevents the overflow problem before it happens.

blocks.title: One live demo.
blocks.body: Show one vague prompt that produces generic output. Then show the specific prompt that produces something usable. The lesson is always the same. Context is everything. Ten minutes, never more. If the demo runs long, the hands-on shrinks.

blocks.title: Hands-on. Walk the room.
blocks.body: Don't troubleshoot from your laptop. Stand up. Walk between people. Look at their actual screens. The room moves because you move. If a project hits something you can't unlock, bring in someone from the wider team who can.

blocks.title: One sentence each.
blocks.body: Round the room. Each person names what they'll do before next session. Single sentence. No discussion. This is how the work continues past the hour.

blocks.title: File the notes.
blocks.body: Open Journal/intake/facilitator-notes.md within 24 hours. Send participants the check-in link — five questions, takes about 10 minutes. The synthesis depends on both.

runbook.callout: The five blocks aren't rigid timekeeping. They give the room a shape — so people know where they are in the hour without you announcing it.

faq.title: Things your team will ask you.
faq.lead: Fifteen questions, in roughly the order you'll hear them. Each answer is tagged so you know whether to read it aloud or make it your own.

paras: The honest answer is the only one that lands. The parts of design AI is best at are the parts most designers like least. Typing out boilerplate. Generating variations. Drafting copy nobody reads carefully. It's not coming for taste. It's not coming for judgment. It's not coming for the part of the work that's about caring.

What it does change: the floor goes up. Worse designers can produce decent-looking output. The ceiling also goes up, by a lot. What a really good designer can make has more headroom than before. The middle gets squeezed.

If they ask what to do about it: get good at the parts AI isn't good at. Taste. Restraint. Knowing what to cut. Telling a client they're wrong. Hearing what the user actually means. None of that is on a roadmap.

items.q: How is this different from copying from Pinterest?

paras: Two ways. First: you're not copying anyone's specific design. AI generates from your prompts, your tokens, your direction. Pinterest is one person's image. AI is a collaborator that builds with your inputs.
Second, and this is the real point: Pinterest stops at the image. AI keeps going. You can ship what you make. The reference is also the deliverable.
If they're worried about derivative output, that's the right worry. It's why the anti-stock principle exists. The techniques in the workshop are how you avoid it.

items.q: I don't know how to code. Is this for me?

paras: You'll know more code at the end of session 12 than you do now, but that's a side effect, not the goal.

The goal is to make a thing you'd be proud to share. Claude writes most of the code. You'll learn to read enough of it to spot when something's wrong, and to change small values yourself when that's faster than re-prompting.
That's it. If you can change a hex code in CSS, you have all the coding skill the workshop requires.

items.q: What if my project is bad?

paras: Most projects wobble around session 6 or 7. The visual direction gets second-guessed, the scope feels wrong, the build looks generic. This is normal. It's not a sign the project is bad.
What to do: don't pivot. Refine.
The instinct to start over almost always loses you a session and lands you somewhere you'd already considered. Discomfort at the midpoint is part of how the work matures.
The exception is if it's genuinely off-track. Wrong scope, wrong scale, doesn't fit them anymore. Say so directly. But check first. Most of the time it's the middle, not the end.
It's not.
The decisions are yours. The taste is yours. The pushback is yours. Claude wrote some words and some lines of code. That's the easiest part of the work, the part that scales with typing speed.
Nobody hires a designer for typing speed. They hire you for everything around the typing. That's still all you.

If they keep pressing on this, and some will, the deeper answer is that "cheating" is a frame from a context where the rules said "do this without help." That's school. This isn't school. The work gets judged by the result, not the process. If the result is good and it's yours, it's yours.

items.q: Why is the AI so confident when it's wrong?

paras: Because it's predicting the next word, and confidence reads as fluent. It doesn't know the difference between knowing and guessing. This is a real limitation, not a bug you can prompt around.

What to do. Never accept Claude's first answer on something that matters. Ask it to argue the opposite case. Ask it to list its assumptions. Ask it what could go wrong. The friction surfaces real thinking.
Also: if a Claude answer feels too clean, that's usually a flag. Reality is messy. Claude's averages aren't.

items.q: How is this not just hype?

paras: Some of it is. There's a lot of breathless content about AI changing everything overnight; most of it is wrong on the timeline and right on the direction.
What's not hype is the practical thing on your screen. Claude can take a Figma comp and build a working site in twenty minutes. That's not a future capability. That's Tuesday.

The workshop isn't betting on the next breakthrough. It's betting that the current capabilities, used well, are already worth twelve hours of attention. By session 12 they'll have shipped something. That's the test.
Read your prompt out loud.

Most prompts that don't work are ambiguous in a way that's invisible when you read silently. Then check three things: did you give Claude the constraints, did you give it the references, did you give it permission to ask clarifying questions.
If two of those are missing, the prompt isn't done yet.
If they're still stuck: the answer is usually to add specificity, not start over. The specificity ladder applies to all prompts, not just builds.

items.q: How much should I trust what Claude says?

rules.head: On facts that change.
rules.body: Verify. Current events, recent prices, the latest version of anything. Claude's training has a cutoff and isn't always honest about what it doesn't know.

rules.head: On opinions and judgments.
rules.body: Treat Claude as one perspective, not the perspective. Useful, not authoritative.

rules.head: On code.
rules.body: Usually accurate enough to use, almost always wrong in some small way. Run it. Test it. Don't trust it on its word.

items.q: Am I going to get behind?

paras: Maybe. And that's okay.
The workshop is designed so each session produces something even if you only show up for the hour. If you fall behind, you don't catch up. You join back in where the group is.
Don't try to make session 4 perfect after the fact while everyone else is on session 6. Lose session 4 cleanly and stay with the group.

items.q: Should I be using Claude every day?

paras: No. Use it when it's useful.
Forcing yourself to use a tool to justify having learned it is the worst kind of habit. The point of the workshop is to build judgment about when AI helps and when it doesn't.
Great for: first drafts, generating variations, getting unstuck, summarising, building mockups quickly.
Bad at: figuring out what you actually want, telling you something you needed to hear, sitting with discomfort.
Notice the difference and act on it.

items.q: I don't want to share my work in the show & tell.

paras: That's fine.

The show & tell isn't a performance review. Skip presenting and just listen if you want to. Show three slides instead of fifteen if you want to. The only thing that's mandatory is showing up. The rest is yours to shape.

But check why. Sometimes "I don't want to share" means "I'm proud of this and afraid of the room." That fear is worth pushing through. If it's genuine privacy or readiness, respect it. Read which one it is.

items.q: Do participants need a Claude account or API access?

paras: They need a Claude account — claude.ai Pro works fine. No API key, no setup beyond that.
Sort out access before session 1, not during it. If someone is blocked on day one, the session loses momentum before it starts.

items.q: How much time should participants expect to put in between sessions?

paras: The session is one hour. That's enough to make real progress.

Time outside the session is optional — but it's also where the projects start to separate. One hour a week and you'll finish. More than that and the work gets good. The more they put in, the better the result. That's a promise, not a pitch.

items.q: Do participants need to read the brief before the session?

paras: Yes. They wrote the brief themselves — it's what they agreed they'd build with Claude. Reading it before the session means they arrive with a plan, not a blank page.
If most of the room hasn't read it, spend five minutes at the top walking it. Don't skip the session content to catch up stragglers.

skeptic.title: Skepticism is information. Read what kind.
skeptic.lead: Small team, mostly new to AI, you'll have at least one. Don't try to convert them. Engage what they're actually saying. There are two flavours. They look similar from the outside. They need different responses. Mistaking one for the other makes both worse.

columns.quote: I don't see how this applies to me.
columns.recognize: Quiet during demos. Doesn't push back, but doesn't engage. Says things like "I'll see how it goes." Detached, not hostile. Shows up to every session and barely speaks.
columns.why: They haven't seen the connection between AI and the work they care about. They're not against it. They just don't have a hook. Forcing engagement makes them more passive, not less.
what: Find their hook. Privately. "What's the part of your job that takes the most time and feels least valuable?"
Whatever they say is their entry point. Build their session-9 automation around that thing. The hook does what argument can't.
Don't pair them with the most enthusiastic person. The energy mismatch is uncomfortable for both. Pair them with someone steady and curious.

columns.quote: This is going to make our work worse.
columns.recognize: Active. Argumentative. Brings up ethics, environment, derivative output, deskilling. Often has a strong opinion already formed. Not detached. Paying close attention.
columns.why: Often a values position, not a tool position. They care about something. Craft, originality, sustainability, a worker's relationship to their tools. They're testing whether the workshop respects that or steamrolls it.
what: Take the position seriously. Don't "both sides" it. Engage the specific point.
"You're right that AI generates a lot of generic output. The whole anti-stock principle exists because that's a real problem. Let's see if the techniques actually solve it for your work."
Don't try to win. The skeptic isn't asking to be persuaded. They're asking to be respected. Workshops that take their concerns seriously usually end up with skeptics as the strongest contributors.
Watch for the moment they engage. It usually comes around session 4 or 5, when they see something work that they couldn't dismiss. Don't celebrate it. Just keep the door open.

skeptic.closing: The thing that ruins this is trying to convert people. Both flavours get worse when you push. Ambivalence becomes withdrawal. Skepticism becomes principled refusal. Don't sell. Don't oversell. Make the work good and let the work do the persuading.

journal.title: Memory fades. The journal is what stays.
journal.lead: The journal isn't a side project. It's how the workshop turns into something you can hand to someone else six months from now — or run again next year without starting from scratch. The system is already set up. Here's how to run it each week.

workflow.title: The workflow.
workflow.intro: Five steps after every session. The first two are yours. The rest runs through Claude.

items.title: File your notes.
items.body: Bullets, half-thoughts, quotes, names, what worked, what didn't. Do it within 24 hours. Don't write for an audience — the synthesis handles shaping. The goal is honest raw material before memory starts editing it.

items.title: Send the check-in.
items.body: Five questions, about 10 minutes per person. Send within 48 hours of the session. Their answers fold into the synthesis. Without them, the entry is half a picture.

items.title: Generate the session report.
items.body: Run the facilitator synthesis prompt from /journal in Claude (model: claude-sonnet-4-6). Paste your notes. It produces a structured session report — what happened, what landed, what fell flat, one line worth keeping. Save to Journal/reports/NN-report.md.

items.title: Synthesise the entry.
items.body: Open Cowork. Run the journal-entry skill with the report as your source. Then run journal-voice for the voice pass — it removes em dashes, shapes the tone, and brings it in line with the rest of the site. Two skills, two passes, done.

items.title: Publish.
items.body: Drop the finished file into content/journal/. Run the build. The entry appears on /journal automatically.

workflow.callout: The session prompts on /journal are pre-filled with each session's title, capability, and expected outputs. Copy and run — they're built to be used directly in Claude.

siteShape.title: What it looks like on the site.
siteShape.intro: Entries live at /journal. Add NN.md to content/journal/, run the build, and it appears automatically. The index, the entry body, and the session prompts are all generated from that one file.

panels.title: The journal index.
panels.body: Reverse-chronological. Each row shows the session number, title, date, and closing quote. A summary block at the top shows where the workshop is up to.

voice.title: The voice.
voice.body: We have a journal voice. Apply it on the way out. The journal-voice skill in Cowork codifies the full ruleset — run it on any draft before it publishes.

journal.closingCallout: The journal is the workshop's most underrated artifact. Twelve weeks from now, the project the team builds will be the visible output. The journal will be the invisible one. And the one you'll quote from for years. Six months from now, when someone asks how the workshop went, you won't summarise from memory. You'll send them the journal.
journal.closingLine: Memory fades. The journal is what stays.

closingLine: You're running a real workshop for real people. It's allowed to be hard.
